project.name: CloudRealm
project.tagline: AWS Fantasy RPG — learn cloud architecture through tabletop board placement

game_world.concept: VPC as walled kingdom; AWS services as fantasy characters and structures

vpc_wall.notes: Structural only. Wall tiles show hatched pattern.

vpc_gate.notes: Special wall tile. Only Internet Gateway can be placed here.

changelog.title: World design and concept
changelog.summary: Initial concept defined in prior session. Established the core fantasy-to-AWS mapping (VPC as kingdom, Lambda as ninja, EC2 as knight, etc.). Produced a world design bible.

changes.description: Defined VPC = kingdom wall concept
Mapped 12 AWS services to fantasy characters/structures
Established public/private subnet zone duality (Outer City vs Inner Sanctum)
Defined gold economy metaphor for AWS compute costs

decisions: Lambda costs 0 gold idle — reflects serverless per-invocation pricing
EC2 costs 20 gold/hr — reflects always-on compute billing
IGW costs 0 gold — reflects real AWS pricing

changelog.title: UI style selection
changelog.summary: Presented four UI directions to the user via visual mockups: Kingdom Map, Side-scrolling RPG, Card Battle, RPG Dashboard. User selected hex tile board.

changes.description: Rejected top-down map, side-scroller, card battle, and dashboard styles
Selected hexagonal tile board — classic tabletop boardgame aesthetic

decisions: Hex tiles chosen over square grid for organic tabletop feel and natural 6-neighbor adjacency
3D board concept preferred — user wants to 'place items onto a board to build their cloud environment'

changelog.title: First playable build — v0.2.0
changelog.summary: Built the complete game as a single HTML file. Hex grid, service palette, drag-and-drop placement, connection lines, gold economy, and 3-mission system.

changes.description: 11×9 hex grid with odd-r offset coordinate system
Three terrain zones: outside, wall/vpc-gate, public subnet, private subnet
12 service cards in left palette with zone badges, cost, and deploy counts
HTML5 drag-and-drop placement (set as primary interaction)
Zone enforcement in canDrop() — services rejected if wrong terrain
Gold economy: deploy costs gold, removal gives 50% refund
SVG connection lines between adjacent compatible services
3 missions with real-time requirement checking
Toast notification system
Tile inspect panel (click to see info)
Zone label overlays (PUBLIC, PRIVATE, VPC WALL)
Cinzel + Crimson Text fantasy typography via Google Fonts
Dark fantasy colour palette: #0d0f14 bg, #c9a84c gold, #4a8c3f green, #2a7a6a teal

bugs_introduced: clip-path on .hex divs blocks pointer events in hexagon corner regions
child elements inside hex tiles absorb dragover/dragleave events before the hex div
drag-and-drop state.dragging can desync during scroll

changelog.title: README created
changelog.summary: Wrote a comprehensive README.md covering concept, gameplay, all 12 services, 3 missions, game mechanics, technical architecture, educational outcomes table, and roadmap.

changes.description: README.md written — ~1800 words covering all game systems
Educational outcomes table: game mechanic → AWS concept → real-world relevance
Technical architecture section: hex CSS approach, drag-and-drop impl, state model
Roadmap section: near/medium/long term features including SAA-C03 prep mode

changelog.title: Placement interaction overhaul — fix unresponsive tiles
changelog.summary: User reported inability to place services on tiles. Root cause analysis identified three stacked bugs. Replaced drag-and-drop with click-select-then-place model. Rewrote hex rendering to fix pointer-events.

changes.description: Moved clip-path from .hex div to inner .hex-bg child div
changes.root_cause: CSS clip-path makes pointer events only fire inside the visible polygon. Hex corners were dead zones that rejected all mouse events.
changes.fix: Outer .hex div is now a plain rectangle (no clip-path) — receives all pointer events reliably. .hex-bg child handles visuals only with pointer-events:none.

changes.description: Added .hex > * { pointer-events: none } to all hex children
changes.root_cause: Child elements (.wall-icon, .gate-icon, .hex-service) were catching dragover/dragleave events, firing spurious dragleave on the parent hex, preventing drop detection.
changes.fix: All hex children set to pointer-events:none via CSS rule. Parent hex div is the sole event target.

changes.description: Replaced HTML5 drag-and-drop with click-select-then-click-place interaction model
changes.root_cause: Drag-and-drop is unreliable across panel boundaries, clip-path, and scrollable containers. state.dragging reference can desync.
changes.fix: New interaction: click service card to select it (gold border highlight), valid tiles pulse animation, invalid tiles dim, click a tile to place, click ✕ or board background to cancel. Much more reliable and discoverable.

changes.description: Added select-hint banner in palette showing currently selected service with cancel button
Valid tile pulse animation (brightness 1.5 → 2.4) and invalid tile dim (opacity 0.45) when service selected
Detailed error toasts explaining why placement failed (wrong zone, tile occupied, max count reached, not enough gold)
Removed all inline onclick= attributes — replaced with addEventListener in renderGrid() and renderMissions()
Added .hex-tint inner div for zone colour overlay, separate from .hex-bg

changelog.title: Notes folder created — change_log.json and implementation_notes.md
changelog.summary: Created this change log and a human-readable engineering walkthrough in the Notes subfolder.

changes.description: Notes/change_log.json — AI-optimised structured project history and state
Notes/implementation_notes.md — human engineer walkthrough with rationale

changelog.title: implementation_techniques.md — DOM vs Canvas analysis
changelog.summary: Analysed two projects (CloudRealm v1 and Dungeon Architect) with different rendering architectures. Documented the DOM vs Canvas tradeoffs, the game loop, delta time, state management patterns, and a 3-phase migration plan.

changes.description: Notes/implementation_techniques.md — 2500-word technical comparison with decision matrix and migration roadmap

changelog.title: Canvas rewrite — cloud-realm/ React + Zustand + RAF
changelog.summary: Full rewrite to Canvas/React/Zustand architecture. Legacy aws-rpg.html preserved for historical reference. New project in cloud-realm/ folder with 11 source files across game/, store/, and components/ directories.

changes.description: cloud-realm/ — npm project with React 18, Zustand 4.5, Vite 5
HTML5 Canvas rendering — hex grid drawn with ctx.beginPath/arc, no DOM nodes for tiles
requestAnimationFrame game loop running at 60fps with delta time support
Zoom: mouse wheel zooms toward cursor using pan = cursor - (cursor - pan) * newZoom/oldZoom
Pan: alt+drag pans the world-space camera
12 programmatic service sprites — all drawn with canvas shapes + Math.sin(t) idle animations
Zustand store — reactive state, zero prop-drilling, store reads via refs in RAF loop
Animated connection lines — flowing dash offset driven by t*0.04
Gate tile glow pulse, wall stripe pattern, valid/invalid tile selection overlays
In-canvas toast notifications (no DOM element, drawn in screen space outside camera transform)
game/ folder is pure functions — no React, no DOM, fully testable in isolation
Simulation/render separation: store handles logic, GameBoard.jsx handles rendering
RAF reads state via refs (gridRef, placedRef, selectedRef) so loop never restarts on state changes

file_structure.game/constants.js: all data — services, missions, grid geometry, terrain colours
file_structure.game/hexGrid.js: hexCenter, pixelToHex, getNeighbors, buildGrid, drawHexPath, canPlaceHere
file_structure.store/gameStore.js: zustand store — grid, placed, selectedServiceId, gold, camera, all actions
file_structure.components/GameBoard.jsx: canvas element + RAF loop + zoom/pan + click handling
file_structure.components/ServicePalette.jsx: HTML left panel — click to select service
file_structure.components/MissionPanel.jsx: HTML right panel — mission requirements, claim button
file_structure.components/HUD.jsx: HTML top bar — gold, deployed count, mission name

v2_active.raf_ref_pattern: gridRef/placedRef/selectedRef/cameraRef updated each render, read inside draw()

design_principles: Every game mechanic must correspond to a real AWS behaviour or constraint
Zone placement rules are never optional — they enforce correct AWS subnet design
Gold cost reflects real AWS billing model (EC2 = always-on cost, Lambda = zero idle cost)
Connection lines are only drawn for architecturally valid service pairs
Single HTML file — zero install friction, runs in any browser
No frameworks — vanilla JS for portability and readability

context_for_ai.how_to_read_this_file: Use changelog array for sequential history. current_state reflects v2 (cloud-realm/). architecture_snapshot.v2_active is the live codebase. v1 (aws-rpg.html) is legacy/read-only.
next_likely_tasks: Add more missions (Route53, Auto Scaling, VPC Peering, ElastiCache)
Add wave/threat mechanics — enemies attack the kingdom, services must be correctly wired to survive
Add walking characters — Lambda ninja moves between tiles on invocation, EC2 knight patrols
Add a title/menu screen and game phase state machine (menu → build → wave → debrief)
Add tutorial overlay for first-time players
Add simulation.js — pure tick function that advances threats, triggers events, checks architecture validity
critical_constraints: All game mechanics must accurately reflect real AWS behaviour
RAF loop reads state ONLY via refs (gridRef, placedRef, cameraRef) — never via closed-over state
game/ folder must remain free of React and DOM — pure functions only
Do NOT modify aws-rpg.html — it is a historical artifact
All new features go in cloud-realm/src/
